summary: I was born and grew up in Laos, where life was very hard for me and my family. My father lived there until he passed away two years ago, and I never got to see him one last time. The Hmong New Year celebrations in Laos are very special and different from here because we celebrate outside in warm weather. I left Laos in 2007 to come to the United States for marriage, bringing with me traditional clothing and memories from my homeland.
cues: father passed away two years ago

summary: I am ethnically Hmong, and my culture is very important to me. My mother sewed traditional Hmong clothes for me before I came to the United States, and these clothes are worn on special occasions like weddings and the Hmong New Year. Many Hmong people live in St. Paul, Minnesota, which makes it feel like home because we share traditions and community. The Hmong New Year is a big celebration that lasts three days, and it's a time when many of us wear our traditional clothes together.

I came to the United States in 2007 by myself to marry my fiancé, leaving my family behind in Laos. Since arriving, I have lived in St. Paul, Minnesota, where there is a large Hmong community and access to Asian foods, which helps me feel connected to my culture. Life here is very different from Laos, especially because of the colder weather and the way we celebrate events like the Hmong New Year indoors. I brought only two suitcases with me, including my traditional clothing, which my mother made for me.

I have lived in St. Paul, Minnesota, since I came to the United States in 2007. I like living here because there are many Hmong people, which makes it easier to maintain my culture and traditions. It’s also convenient to find Asian foods and go to school. St. Paul feels like a supportive place for me as I adjust to life away from my family in Laos.

The Hmong New Year is a very important event for me and my community. In Laos, we celebrate it outside over three days, and it’s more fun there because the weather is warm. Here in the United States, the celebration is different because it is colder and often indoors, but many Hmong people still wear traditional clothes to honor the occasion. This event brings us together to celebrate our culture and heritage, just like weddings where traditional Hmong clothing is also worn.
